skill.name: flux-2-klein
skill.description: Generate Flux 2 Klein images through the RunComfy CLI with model-specific prompting guidance for fast concepts, polished outputs, and reference-based styling.
skill.summary: Create fast Flux 2 Klein images on RunComfy with practical prompt and variant guidance.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, and filesystem findings are mostly true indicators of the skill's intended RunComfy workflow, not evidence of malware. The skill should publish with warnings because it invokes a local CLI, sends user prompts and reference assets to a third-party API, stores or reads a RunComfy token, and writes generated files locally.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution for Image Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run the local RunComfy CLI with user-provided prompt JSON. This is an intended workflow, but it executes an external command and depends on the installed CLI behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and repeated, and the How it works section confirms CLI execution. The file also states prompt content is passed as JSON, so command injection is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Submission of User Content
medium_findings.description: Prompts, reference images, and bearer-token-authenticated requests are sent to RunComfy model endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but users must understand the privacy boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill names the RunComfy API endpoint and states that external image, mask, or video URLs are fetched by the model server. This confirms third-party data processing, but not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Local Token Storage and Output File Writes
medium_findings.description: The RunComfy login flow stores an API token under the user home config directory and generated assets are downloaded into the selected output directory. This is normal CLI behavior but increases filesystem sensitivity.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file documents both output downloads and token storage path directly. It also states owner-only permissions and a download size cap, reducing but not eliminating risk.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives from Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, path traversal, and system reconnaissance hits are Markdown formatting, model names, placeholder paths, exit-code text, or URL ellipses. No evidence found that the skill contains executable Ruby, cryptographic operations, reconnaissance code, or path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the surrounding lines shows documentation prose and shell examples, not source code implementing those patterns. The remaining real behavior is captured in the medium findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: runcomfy CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on commands such as runcomfy run with JSON input and an output directory. This pattern requires user trust in the installed CLI and careful handling of prompt values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is clearly documented and central to the skill. The prompt is JSON encoded, so this is a controlled external-command pattern rather than confirmed injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token and Remote Download Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow submits authenticated requests to RunComfy, polls for results, and downloads generated files from whitelisted RunComfy hosts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network and credential flow is documented explicitly in the skill. The described host allowlist and file-size cap reduce evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Fast image iteration is hard when model choice and prompt structure are unclear. This skill routes requests to Flux 2 Klein on RunComfy and gives practical prompt patterns for faster, cleaner results.
actual_capabilities: Invokes Flux 2 Klein 4B or 9B through the local RunComfy CLI.
Provides prompt structure guidance for subject-first image prompts.
Explains when to use 4B for fast concepts and 9B for polished outputs.
Documents width, height, step count, and prompt-length limits.
Supports reference-image workflows as documented by the RunComfy model API.
Suggests routing to other image models when Flux 2 Klein is a poor fit.
limitations: Requires a RunComfy account and either login state or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN.
Sends prompts and reference assets to RunComfy model infrastructure.
Does not provide native 4K output or extreme aspect ratios.
Depends on the installed RunComfy CLI and current model API behavior.

use_cases.title: Rapid Product Concepts
use_cases.description: Create quick product visualization drafts before investing in a polished render.

use_cases.title: Brand Style Exploration
use_cases.description: Use aligned reference images to test consistent visual directions across campaign assets.

use_cases.title: Live Art Direction
use_cases.description: Iterate on composition, lighting, and styling during a fast creative review session.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Flux 2 Klein 4B to generate a fast concept image of [subject] in [setting] with [style]. Save it to [output folder].

prompt_templates.title: Polish a Final Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Flux 2 Klein 9B with about 25 steps. Create [subject], [action], [scene], [lighting], [camera], and [quality details].

prompt_templates.title: Match Reference Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Flux 2 Klein with these reference images: [paths or URLs]. Keep the same visual style, but change [specific element] to [new element].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Iteration Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate three Flux 2 Klein 4B concepts for [brief]. Choose the strongest direction, then make one 9B polished version with refined lighting and composition.

output_examples.input: A designer asks for a fast poster concept with a clean product scene.
output_examples.output: The skill selects the 4B variant, builds a subject-first prompt, runs RunComfy, and returns the saved image location.

output_examples.input: A brand team provides two references and asks for a matching bottle label image.
output_examples.output: The skill keeps the reference style aligned, describes what should stay fixed, changes the label, and uses the RunComfy model endpoint.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a 4K hero image with dense multilingual text.
output_examples.output: The skill explains the Flux 2 Klein limits and recommends a better model for high resolution or precise embedded text.

best_practices: Use the 4B variant for quick exploration and the 9B variant for final polish.
Keep prompts under about 512 tokens with the subject first and concrete visual details.
Use consistent reference images when asking for brand or style alignment.
anti_patterns: Do not include confidential prompts or private reference images unless RunComfy processing is acceptable.
Do not request 4K output, extreme aspect ratios, or more than four reference images.
Do not mix conflicting styles or vague adjectives in the same image prompt.

faq.question: Does this skill run Flux 2 Klein locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the local RunComfy CLI to call RunComfy hosted model endpoints.

faq.question: Which variant should I use first?
faq.answer: Use 4B for fast concepts. Use 9B when the direction is chosen and quality matters more.

faq.question: Can it use reference images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents reference-image use through the RunComfy model API, with a practical limit of four references.

faq.question: Does it need credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. RunComfy requires login state or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable for authenticated requests.

faq.question: Is it safe for private assets?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and referenced assets are processed by RunComfy infrastructure, not only on your machine.

faq.question: Can it create exact in-image text?
faq.answer: It can try simple text, but the skill recommends another model for heavy typography or multilingual signage.
